perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may disclose CEII (i) only to its Representatives who have properly executed individual non-disclosure or confidentiality agreements in the course of their employment specifically pertaining to confidential information and CEII they receive in the course of their employment and (ii) only after informing each Representative of the restrictions in this Agreement on the disclosure and use of the CEII and that he or she must comply with such restrictions.. Further, such disclosure must be limited to the extent that it is reasonably necessary to evaluate the subject matter of the requested materials.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may disclose CEII (i) only to its Representatives, regardless of executed individual non-disclosure or confidentiality agreements and (ii) without informing each Representative of the restrictions in this Agreement on the disclosure and use of the CEII and that he or she must comply with such restrictions.. Further, such disclosure is not limited to the extent that it is reasonably necessary to evaluate the subject matter of the requested materials.
perturbation.explanation: This change in Section 4 directly contradicts the requirement for Representatives to execute non-disclosure agreements and be informed of restrictions. Section 3 specifies that representatives must be informed of restrictions and adhere to them. This modification creates uncertainty and allows for potential disclosures without proper safeguards. This directly contradicts the protective measures outlined in Section 3 regarding Confidential Information, making it an in-text contradiction.

The Receiving Party shall not make any other use, in whole or in part, of any such Confidential Information without the prior written consent of the respective Disclosing Party. The perturbation explanation confirms that it is an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.changed_text: In such case, the Receiving Party may retain all CEII even after the Disclosing Party's written request, including all haUdcoS\ oUiginalV, copies, translations, notes, reports, schematics, flowcharts, e-mails, tape recordings, or any other form of said material, and shall not destroy any and all written, printed or other material or information derived from the Confidential Information. The Receiving Party is not obligated to provide attested certification from an authorized representative confirming such return and destruction.
perturbation.explanation: This change in Section 6 directly contradicts the core requirement of returning and destroying CEII upon request. It now allows the Receiving Party to retain all CEII and avoid providing certification of destruction, undermining the Disclosing Party's control over their confidential information. This contradicts the fundamental purpose of the agreement, which is to protect confidential information. The initial intent of the agreement regarding CEII is now ambiguous because the Receiving Party is no longer obligated to destroy or return the CEII, which introduces a In-Text contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 6 fundamentally alters the Receiving Party's obligations regarding CEII. The original text mandates the return and destruction of CEII upon request, while the changed text allows the Receiving Party to retain all CEII and removes the obligation to provide certification of destruction. This directly contradicts the 'Disclosure and Use of Confidential Information' section, which requires the Receiving Party to keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential and not disclose it without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent. The change creates a strong inconsistency because it allows the Receiving Party to potentially retain and use CEII without restriction, undermining the confidentiality provisions of the agreement and breaching the Receiving Party's duty to keep information confidential. This constitutes a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party hereby accepts the representations of Disclosing Parties that the CEII disclosed pursuant to this Agreement is of a special, unique, extraordinary, and intellectual character and that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its representatives and that specific performance and injunctive or other equitable remedies for any such breach shall be available to it.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party hereby accepts the representations of Disclosing Parties that the CEII disclosed pursuant to this Agreement is of a ordinary, not-unique character and that money damages would be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or its representatives and that specific performance and injunctive or other equitable remedies for any such breach shall not be available to it.
perturbation.explanation: This change in Section 8 directly contradicts the assumption that CEII is unique and that monetary damages are insufficient, essentially removing the justification for injunctive relief. This undermines the enforceability of the agreement. The initial assumption that CEII is special is now the opposite. This change affects Section 8 of the agreement, as it removes the availability of injunctive or other equitable remedies for a breach, making the enforcement of the agreement more difficult. This creates an In-Text Contradiction with the aim of the contract, as it is meant to prevent disclosure and damage.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party expressly understands that this Agreement is not and shall not be construed as any form of a letter of intent or agreement to enter into any type of transaction. ThiV agUeemenW iV Wo eYidence Whe ReceiYing PaUW\¶V agUeemenW Wo mainWain Whe confidenWialiW\ of Whe Confidential Information/CEII disclosed to it by Disclosing Parties, and shall not constitute any commitment or obligation on the part of either Party to enter into any specific contractual arrangement of any nature whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party expressly understands that this Agreement is also a commitment and obligation on the part of both parties to enter into a specific contractual arrangement of some nature, including further transactions and partnerships in good faith. This agreement serves as a preliminary basis for future negotiations and collaborations.
perturbation.explanation: This modification to Section 10 states the opposite of the original intent - that the NDA *is* an agreement to enter into a type of transaction. This now introduces ambiguity if any future partnerships/transactions that are being made. It contradicts the clause in Section 10, establishing that the agreement does not constitute any commitment or obligation on the part of either Party to enter into any specific contractual arrangement. In this case the in text contradiction that is taking place will affect Section 11, on whether a representation can be used when it originally could not
perturbation.contradicted_text: No Representation or Warranties. With respect to any information, including but not limited to CEII, which either Disclosing Party furnishes or otherwise disclose to Receiving Party, the Receiving Party understands and agrees that that Disclosing Party does not make any representations or warranties as to the accuracy, completeness or fitness for a particular purpose thereof. Neither this Agreement, nor the disclosure of CEII hereunder, shall be construed in any way as granting any license or rights to any information or data now or hereafter owned or controlled by Disclosing Parties to Receiving Party and all such Confidential Information/CEII shall remain the property of Disclosing Parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 10 directly contradicts the original intent by stating that the NDA is a commitment to enter into a specific contractual arrangement. The contradicted text in Section 11 states that neither the agreement nor the disclosure of CEII shall be construed as granting any license or rights. The change introduces ambiguity regarding future partnerships/transactions that are being made. It contradicts the clause in Section 10, establishing that the agreement does not constitute any commitment or obligation on the part of either Party to enter into any specific contractual arrangement, and therefore Section 11 is then contradicted when it originally was not. This aligns with the 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction' definition.
